class_schedule.topic: The Medium Is the Message: Literature in the Age of Media
learning_objectives: Analyze McLuhan's thesis that media shape perception and consciousness
Understand remediation: how new media incorporate and transform older media
Identify how literature has responded to and been transformed by film, television, and digital culture
readings: McLuhan, Understanding Media, Ch. 1 ('The Medium Is the Message')

lecture_notes.introduction: Every new medium transforms existing ones. Film changed how novels handle time and space. Television changed narrative structure. The internet is changing everything. This course examines the relationship between literature and media — not as competitors but as mutually transforming systems.
key_concepts: McLuhan's thesis: the medium shapes the message more than content does
Remediation: new media incorporate old media (film remediates theater, websites remediate print)
Adaptation as interpretation: every film adaptation is a reading of the source text
The novel and cinema: how cinematic techniques (montage, close-up, cross-cutting) entered fiction
The digital turn: how hypertext, social media, and streaming reshape narrative expectations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing how a single scene works differently in a novel, its film adaptation, and a streaming TV version — showing what each medium can do that the others cannot

review.value: McLuhan's claim that the most important thing about any medium is not its content but how it restructures human perception, communication, and social organization

The process by which new media incorporate, absorb, and transform the conventions of older media — film remediates theater and photography, television remediates film, and digital media remediate everything that came before

class_schedule.topic: Adaptation: From Page to Screen and Back
learning_objectives: Analyze film adaptation as interpretation, not mere translation
Identify what is gained and lost when narrative moves between media
Understand fidelity criticism and its limitations
readings: Hutcheon, A Theory of Adaptation, Ch. 1-2
Watch: one assigned adaptation before class

lecture_notes.introduction: Every adaptation is an interpretation. The question is not whether it is 'faithful' to the source but what it reveals about both the source text and the capabilities of the new medium.
key_concepts: Fidelity criticism: the demand that adaptations be faithful to the source — and why this demand is conceptually flawed
Medium specificity: what novels can do that films cannot (interiority, narrative voice) and vice versa (visual spectacle, simultaneity)
The adapter as interpreter: directorial choices as acts of criticism
Transmedia storytelling: narratives that unfold across multiple media platforms
The feedback loop: how successful adaptations change how we read the original
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing the opening of a novel with its film adaptation, analyzing how the director translates narrative voice, interiority, and description into visual and aural equivalents

review.value: The common demand that an adaptation be 'faithful' to its source assumes that the source has a single correct meaning that should be preserved — but every reading is an interpretation, and adaptation simply makes the interpretation visible

Each medium has unique capabilities: novels excel at interiority and narrative voice; film excels at simultaneity and visual composition; neither is superior, and adaptation involves compensating for what is lost by exploiting what is gained

class_schedule.topic: The Graphic Novel: Visual Storytelling and the Literary
learning_objectives: Analyze the formal conventions of the graphic novel: panel composition, gutters, page layout
Understand how visual and verbal elements interact to create meaning in comics
Evaluate the graphic novel's contested status as literature

lecture_notes.introduction: The graphic novel combines two semiotic systems — words and images — in a sequential format that is neither illustrated text nor captioned pictures but a distinct medium with its own grammar and rhetoric.
key_concepts: Sequential art: meaning is created in the relationship between panels, not within individual images
The gutter: the space between panels where the reader's imagination fills in the gap
Panel composition: size, shape, and arrangement as rhetorical devices
Maus: using the comics medium (funny animal characters) for Holocaust testimony — the dissonance as meaning
The literary graphic novel: how texts like Maus, Persepolis, and Fun Home challenge the boundary between comics and literature
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of a page from Maus, analyzing how Spiegelman uses panel size, visual metaphor (mice and cats), and the interaction of image and text to create meaning that neither words nor pictures could achieve alone

review.value: McCloud's concept: the reader's act of mentally filling in the action between panels. The gutter — the blank space between panels — is where the reader's imagination does the narrative work, making comics a uniquely participatory medium

Spiegelman's use of animal characters (Jews as mice, Germans as cats) draws on the propaganda tradition of dehumanizing caricature and simultaneously critiques it — the reader can never forget that these are people drawn as animals, maintaining an uncomfortable awareness of the representation's artificiality

class_schedule.topic: Television and the Novel: The Rise of Prestige TV
learning_objectives: Analyze how contemporary television serial narrative borrows and transforms novelistic techniques
Identify what long-form serial storytelling can do that neither the novel nor film can
Understand the cultural implications of literature's declining centrality in narrative culture
readings: Watch: assigned prestige TV episode before class

lecture_notes.introduction: In the 21st century, television has arguably replaced the novel as the dominant narrative art form. Long-form serial television — from The Wire to Succession — uses techniques the novel pioneered (character development, social panorama, moral ambiguity) in a medium that reaches far larger audiences.
key_concepts: Complex television: serial narrative that rewards sustained attention and rewards rewatching
The television showrunner as novelist: long-form character development across seasons
What TV can do that novels cannot: simultaneity, visual composition, performance, soundtrack
What novels can do that TV cannot: interiority, unreliable narration, linguistic texture
The death of the novel (again): is literature losing its cultural centrality, or transforming?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a scene from a prestige TV series alongside a comparable scene from a novel to identify how each medium handles the same narrative problem differently

review.value: Mittell's term for TV narratives that combine serial continuity with episodic structure, demand active audience engagement, and employ narrative techniques (flashbacks, unreliable narrators, parallel storylines) previously associated with literary fiction

The question is not whether literature will survive but what it will become: freed from the obligation to be the dominant narrative medium, the novel may discover new possibilities in what only it can do — interiority, linguistic density, and the individual reader's imagination

class_schedule.topic: Digital Literature and the Future of the Book
learning_objectives: Analyze forms of digital literature: hypertext fiction, electronic poetry, interactive narrative
Understand how digital media challenge fundamental literary concepts: authorship, linearity, the fixed text
Evaluate claims about the death or transformation of the book
readings: Explore: Electronic Literature Collection, Volume 3 (selected works)

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital literature is not literature on a screen. It is literature that could not exist without the computational medium — works that use interactivity, generativity, and multimodality as literary resources. It challenges everything we think we know about what a text is.
key_concepts: Hypertext fiction: narratives with multiple paths that the reader navigates through choice
Electronic poetry: poems that use animation, interactivity, sound, and code as poetic elements
Generative literature: texts created or modified by algorithms, never the same twice
The death of the author (digital remix): when readers can rearrange, remix, or contribute to a text
The codex persists: why physical books survive despite digital alternatives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Exploring two works of electronic literature in class, discussing how interactivity and multimodality create literary experiences impossible in print

review.value: Works with an important literary aspect that take advantage of the capabilities of the stand-alone or networked computer — not digitized print literature but literature that requires the digital medium to exist

Despite decades of predictions, the physical book endures — not because digital is inferior but because the codex offers specific affordances (durability, browsability, marginalia, no battery required) that digital has not fully replicated

questions.question: Why is 'fidelity criticism' — judging an adaptation by how faithful it is to the source — considered conceptually flawed? What should replace it?
questions.answer: Fidelity criticism assumes the source text has one correct meaning that should be preserved, but every reading is already an interpretation. No adaptation can be 'faithful' because there is no single fixed original to be faithful to. Instead, we should analyze adaptation as interpretation: what does the adapter's choices reveal about the source, and what new meanings does the new medium create?

rubric.key: Identifies the flawed assumption about fixed meaning
Explains why fidelity is conceptually impossible

questions.question: What does McLuhan mean by 'the medium is the message'? Give one example from literature and media.
questions.answer: McLuhan argues that the most significant effect of any medium is not its content but how it restructures human perception and social organization. For example, the shift from oral to print culture did not simply deliver the same stories in a new format — it created new forms of thought (linear argument), new social structures (mass literacy, individual reading), and new literary possibilities (the novel as a form designed for solitary, sustained reading).

rubric.key: Demonstrates understanding of medium vs. content distinction

questions.question: What is 'closure' in comics as defined by Scott McCloud, and why is it essential to how the medium creates meaning?
questions.answer: Closure is the reader's act of mentally filling in the action that occurs between panels — in the gutter, the blank space that separates one panel from the next. It is essential because comics show only selected moments; the reader's imagination must connect them into continuous action. This makes comics a uniquely participatory medium: the reader is a co-creator of the narrative, not a passive recipient.

rubric.key: Explains the role of the gutter
Identifies reader participation as the key feature

questions.question: What can long-form television serial narrative do that the novel cannot? Give one specific example.
questions.answer: Television can deploy performance, visual composition, soundtrack, and editing simultaneously in real time — creating meaning through the interaction of multiple semiotic channels at once. For example, a TV series can show a character's face saying one thing while the soundtrack, lighting, and other characters' reactions communicate something entirely different — a density of simultaneous meaning that the novel, operating through sequential language, cannot replicate.

rubric.key: Identifies a specific capability unique to television
Explains why the novel cannot achieve this

major_assignments.title: Media Analysis Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,500-3,000 word essay analyzing a specific case of literature's interaction with another medium: an adaptation, a graphic novel, a transmedia narrative, or a work of electronic literature. The essay must use media theory and close analysis of both the literary and non-literary texts.
objectives: Analyze the interaction between literature and another medium with theoretical sophistication
Demonstrate close reading skills applicable to both verbal and visual/digital texts
Integrate media theory as an analytical framework
Evaluate what is gained and lost in medial transfer or hybridization
deliverables: Works Cited with primary texts across media and at least 3 scholarly sources
Media comparison appendix: side-by-side analysis of a key scene or passage across media

criteria: Analysis addresses medium-specific features, not just content
Comparison reveals what each medium contributes uniquely
Analysis avoids privileging one medium over another
Verbal text analysis is detailed and accurate
Analysis connects formal features to meaning across media
Evidence is specific and well-chosen from both media
Complex media-theoretical concepts are explained clearly
Sophisticated analysis of literature's interaction with another medium
Detailed close reading across verbal and visual/digital texts

media_analysis_essay.description: Essay on literature's interaction with another medium

quizzes.description: Quizzes on media theory and assigned texts

final_project.description: Creative-critical project: produce a small-scale adaptation, transmedia piece, or media analysis with scholarly essay

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction.
course_policies.collaboration: All written work is individual. Group viewing and discussion is collaborative.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism results in a zero and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop for in-class digital literature exploration, access to streaming services for assigned viewing, word processing software, library database access, and LMS access.
